church_all_nations_jerusalem.description: A dark, mosaic-lined basilica at the Garden of Gethsemane; the interior is designed for quiet rather than brightness.
facts: Also known as the Basilica of the Agony.
Stands beside ancient olive trees in the garden.
church_all_nations_jerusalem.history: Built after World War I over layers of earlier chapels associated with pilgrimage traditions.
church_all_nations_jerusalem.significance: One of the most visited Christian sites in the city’s pilgrimage circuit.

dormition_abbey_jerusalem.description: A solid stone abbey with a rounded dome near the Old City walls; a calm stop near Zion Gate.
facts: Associated with traditions of Mary’s Dormition.
dormition_abbey_jerusalem.history: Built as a Benedictine abbey during late Ottoman and early modern Jerusalem transformations.
dormition_abbey_jerusalem.significance: A major Mount Zion church stop connecting medieval and modern pilgrimage routes.

lutheran_redeemer_church_jerusalem.description: A prominent tower you can climb for Old City rooftops; the church sits amid dense market streets.
facts: Known for tower views over the Holy Sepulchre area.
A quieter interior relative to nearby pilgrimage crowds.
lutheran_redeemer_church_jerusalem.history: Built as a Protestant landmark in the late 19th-century renewal of Christian Quarter institutions.
lutheran_redeemer_church_jerusalem.significance: A key Protestant church in the Old City with a practical viewpoint bonus.

st_annes_church_jerusalem.description: A remarkably intact crusader-era church with famous acoustics; often hosts short choral performances.
facts: Known for clean stone vaults and strong acoustics.
Located near the Pools of Bethesda area.
st_annes_church_jerusalem.history: Built in the crusader period and preserved through later centuries with relatively few alterations.
st_annes_church_jerusalem.significance: One of the best-preserved Romanesque churches in the region.

st_peter_gallicantu_jerusalem.year_built: Modern church (20th c.) over earlier tradition site
st_peter_gallicantu_jerusalem.description: A hillside church with city views and a sequence of chapels; a reflective stop on walks between Zion and the Valley.
facts: Views across the Kidron and Hinnom valleys.
Often less crowded than central Old City shrines.
st_peter_gallicantu_jerusalem.history: Built over a site associated by tradition with Peter’s denial; the complex incorporates archaeological features.
st_peter_gallicantu_jerusalem.significance: A meaningful pilgrimage stop that also works well as a quiet viewpoint.
